summary: This notebook serves as a tutorial for the March Mania 2025 competition by dissecting the 1st place solution from March Mania 2023. It walks through essential college basketball domain knowledge, data loading, and a step-by-step breakdown of feature engineering, model training, and probability calibration. Readers learn how to transform raw game logs into a compact feature set, train an XGBoost model to predict point differentials, and apply a spline-based calibration to convert margins into win probabilities. The tutorial emphasizes that a small, well-crafted feature set combined with careful post-processing can be highly effective for this type of tabular prediction task.
competition_flow: Loads historical regular season and tournament CSVs, engineers ~45 team-level and matchup features, trains an XGBoost model to predict point differentials, calibrates predictions with a spline to generate win probabilities, and outputs a submission CSV.
data_reading: pd.read_csv with os.walk, fallback to cp1252 encoding, concatenating M/W tournament and regular season CSVs
data_processing: Swaps winner/loser columns to double dataset size, standardizes column prefixes to T1_/T2_, encodes location as integers (1/-1/0), clips predictions to [-30, 30] and final probabilities to [0.025, 0.975]
features_engineering: Aggregated regular season box score stats (FGM, FGA, 3PM, OR, Ast, TO, Stl, PF) by team/season
GLM-derived team quality scores using binomial family
ensembling: Averages predictions across 3 model repeats and applies a UnivariateSpline calibration to map point differentials to win probabilities, with final probability clipping.
insights: Predicting point differentials rather than win probabilities directly can be more effective for this competition.
A small set of carefully engineered features (~45) is sufficient when combined with proper calibration.
Retraining on the full dataset for inference often yields better performance than relying solely on CV out-of-fold predictions.
critical_findings: The spline calibration curve shows slight inversion at the tails, which the author notes as a potential issue.
XGBoost version differences (0.81 vs 2.0.3) can significantly alter predictions and final scores, requiring environment awareness.
